topic: Unit 2: Listening, Reading and Use of Spanish
subject: spanish

items.q: How do you write 'Christmas' and 'Easter' in Spanish?
items.correct: Navidad y Pascua (note: Semana Santa for Holy Week traditions).
items.why: Students add unnecessary accents or use English. Navidad has NO accent (stress on the final syllable but ends in -d which is naturally stressed). Pascua follows normal stress rules. Customs vocab: Reyes Magos (Three Kings), feria (fair).

items.q: What is the Spanish word for 'mobile phone' (in Spain)?
items.misconception: El celular (which is used in Latin America).
items.why: Vocabulary varies by region. Spain: móvil, ordenador (computer), coche (car). Latin America: celular, computadora, carro/auto. Students mix these. Examiners accept both, but consistency matters. Móvil is most common in GCSE/A-level.

A notorious faux ami that causes real confusion. 'Estoy embarazada' = 'I am pregnant'. For 'embarrassed' use 'avergonzado/a'. Native speakers laugh at this one - learners avoid by association with shame.

items.q: How many diputados sit in the Congreso?
items.why: It's 350. “230” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which is the future of 'votar' for 'él'?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: votará. “votó” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was the current Spanish Constitution adopted?
items.why: The real figure is 1978. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
